div.p: an elephant that used to live near
metathesis ʔupi´rtakä´ni turn into; become v.T.tr = ʔupi´rakatä´ni turn into; become v.T.tr.
ta´htc'ic hona'ata = the sun's going down
hi´ku˳arɔwa = tigre blanc (he's white and lives in the water)

tr.td: when he wanted (was ready) to shoot him
when he wanted (was ready) to shoot him
if I talk to both of you
if I talk to both of you
he swallowed them and ate them
he comes and swallows me but
he swallowed them and ate them
he comes and swallows me but
he is in the habit of coming
he is in the habit of coming
he leaned against (the tree) and stood
he leaned against (the tree) and stood
(out) flying he got out (of the water)
(out) flying he got out (of the water)

div.p: se´hitic ta´hka 'i´cunik'ahtca = tomorrow I'm going to shuck corn
se´hiti wi´lanik'ahtca = I'm going to weave tomorrow
How They Made Hats from Palmetto Leaves
'a´hpasala´mihta = an animal that lived in the water

tr.td: he was stretched out (on the ground)
just like a quilt, the size of a quilt
he was stretched out (on the ground)
just like a quilt, the size of a quilt

div.p: ku´ledigri = Fr. name (prob.) Name of pond

tr.td: the beasts the two of them
she passed him, went ahead of him
the beasts the two of them
she passed him, went ahead of him

div.p: For words in the " Coon " Dance referred
to on opposite page, see V 14-16.
How They Danced the Raccon Dance
How and What the Indians Used to Hunt
ka´nahku la´p'iwa´na = I want to buy something
ka´nahk'ula´p'iwana = I want to sell something
wi´nimą wą̈cka wįkä´ck'aki = you (2m.) sprained your feet
si´nimą säcka sįkäck'aki = they (2f.) sprained their feet
sä´mą sä´cka sįkäck'aki = they (m. pl.) sprained their feet
tcu´hkiri´sa = (scrub?) oak, a small kind of oak
does not know any word for " swamp " except " ta´Rkukitc " = in the woods, muhtul n (see Swn.
ta´rihkuwi´lak ka´l'ura = the elm-tree is standing
S objects that Swn isolates rɔwac n from rihku n
ta´watoru´hki ka´l'ura = the heron is standing
S doesn't know the word for " fox, renard " in his language.
He says tcula n is Choctaw, not Tunica (see Swn p 8)
ka´na yu´k'akic 'e´rus´aha = Something I used to (know) I don't know
lɔ´hkame´limiliri´sato´hku = basket black and red mixed
'ihni´yup'aha = I haven't no sense; (that's the only way you can say "crazy")
'i´ht'ira si´hinapan'ɔhɔ = my clothes are not very clean
'i´ht'ira ta´hkir'ähä = my clothes are not dirty
'u´wi la´spi 'uyu´w'ahkini = I gave him some money
se´hiti la´spi 'uyuwanik'ahtca = Tomorrow I'm going to give him money
se´hiti la´spi 'iyu´wawik'a´htca = tomorrow he's going to give me money
ra´p'uhpe´ri na´'uwa = he's gone to sleep
'u´wi ti´rapawik'ahtca = he's going to kill her
to´nik ha´hka ta´pawąhi˳´aku = he intends (? inf says want) to plant corn
to´nik 'u´hahkac wa´hkak'ura = the man is breaking his corn
to´nik 'u´hahkac 'a´kan'ura = the man is getting his corn in
se´hitic 'u´hahkac 'ak'utak'ahtca = tomorrow he's going to get his corn in
'a´kawik'a´htca = he's going to get (his corn) in
to´nik 'u´hahkac 'a´k'uwana = the man wants to get his corn in
to´nik 'u´hahkac 'akawą ya´ku = the man intends ('is trying to', inf. 's explanation here) to get his corn in
to´nik 'u´hahkac wahkayawą hi˳aku se´hiti = the man intends to break his corn tomorrow
" tcuru adj " is unkown to S.,
ti´cuh ti´r˳ic = in front of the door
ta´hiku = he puts the string on (the bow)
to´nik 'u´hahka ta´p'uhki = the man planted his corn
hį˳ati ta´yimohku ra´ti = then the grass grew
'u´hahka po´ht'uhki = and he hoes his corn.
ra´wąhi˳aku = it's trying to grow (inf. says "it's going to grow")
ta´yimohku ra´hk'ara = the grass is growing
ta´hkak sa´mahk'ura = the corn is getting ripe
ta´hkak sa´mawik'ahtca = the corn is going to get ripe
ta´hkak sa´map'u´hki = the corn is already ripe
ta´yimo´hku sa´m'aki = the grass is ripe
ta´yimo´hku sa´man'ara = the grass is getting ripe
ta´yimo´hku sa´map'a´ki = the grass is already ripe
ta´tcuhkitä´racki sa´masiti pa´tasiti = the oak leaves ripen + fall
'ä´racki = the big part of the lower log
ta´tcuhkitä´racki sa´masiti mi´li˳asiti = the oak-leaves ripen and turn red.
ra´mąto le´huku = he talks (? throws up) slow
pa´laku = he catches (prob. in the restricted sense of catching game)
to´nikanahya´nawic 'o´rus'aha = the man something he speaks he doesn't know (in ans. to "he tells a lie")
te´hkunak 'ikähtc'uhki ro´kukati = the mosquito bit me, it burns
ta´cilami´lisį 'ikähtc'ɔnasiti ci´takati = the red bugs bit me, it's itching
'i´mą ha´hpaya ya´kani = I'm making a noise
'u´wį ha´hpaya ya´ku = he is making noise
ka´na hi´lakati wi´hk'atą = I hear something moving
'uya´citik'ähä´ = he's not going to get mad
'i´mą wi´n'anik'ahtca = I'm going to hear it.
'uwį wi´'uta k'a´htca = he's going to hear it
ro´ptin'ɔ´cka = the cotton stalk or root (cotton his feet)
ta´tirata'u´kinik 'u´ra = the button lies (the gender of this word bothers the inf.; he says it is not a "he" or a "she" either one, so you can say 'ara or 'ura either one)
'uha´icik n (S˳ says must be " the Lord ")
he´riku = he (rabbit) knocks on the ground and says "kwą̈, kwą̈, kwą̈" "pɔk, pɔk, pɔk" he knock on the ground
This is a sign somebody's going to die, the old folks said

tr.td: he wanted to see what's going to become of him, how smart he is
he wanted to see what's going to become of him, how smart he is

div.p: * It seems to me that this word should be omitted.
does not seem to notice any difference whether it is put in or not, so I don't know.
ta´hkɔrha´iht = on top of the table
ta´hkɔraha´iht = on top of the table
ta´wicha´ihta = on top of the water
ta´wicänaha´luhta = the bottom of the lake
hopi´wik'a´htca = he's going to go out

tr.td: when he took hold of it
when he took hold of it

div.p: * Not necessary to have this said twice, according to inf.

tr.td: he hit it and broke it
he hit it and broke it
what you doing? how you do?
what you doing? how you do?

div.p: doubtless all the cases where it is written ta´hkuk n should be ta´Rkuk n
'ura'ɔhkini = I put it down (of box of clips)
'uwipą 'u´ra'ɔ´hkini = I put it down (of small basket)
mu´raku = (inf. is unable to explain what this means, see ex. clove)
Correct places where it is written 'o´nmahųsä´mą
'u´gatcic tihta´m'una = he's living with his mother
ti´htcį tihta´t'ä 'uta´makati = she associates with her brother
ti´kik 'uhta´m'a´tci = she's living with her uncle
The Origin of the Pole-Bean (second telling)

tr.td: they were running to get (on the bank)
they were running to get (on the bank)

div.p: 'i´mą tipa´lanik'a´htca = I'm going to win her
'i´mą tiyɔ´lanik'a´htca = I'm going to leave her
'i´mą tile´htanik'a´htca = I'm going to lose her
ka´pac'uhku to´hkuh'i´ti = we strike two eggs together
ka´pac'uhku to´hk'iti = we strike two eggs together
ta´wicic si´hutik'ahtca = the water is going to dry it
se´hitic ta´wicic ya´r'atak'ahtca = the water's going to go down tomorrow
ya´ratik'ahtca = she's going to go down
ta´wici wi´tcitik'a´htca = the water's going to rise
ta´˳imuhkeri ta´pɔhtak ce´huku = the boat's boiler steams (whistles, blows off)
ta´kotyuki tihi´lahk'atani = I'm stirring the hominy
takapac wa´likati = she calls the chicken
ta´kapac ka´pac'uhk ra´hk'ɔki = the hen laid an egg
ta´kapac'upul chicken feathers n ta´lac arrow n
ta´yųkacu´htci (string) thread for sewing tcu´ni I have taken
ta´lac the arrow ta´roptį cotton n
'uhte´hįrihtcuka´ni = put it all around and I tie it (with thread)
'i´yut'ä'o´hkų ka´pac'ähä = a pig is not a chicken
to´nicik ka´pac 'ula´kaha = that man has no chickens
ti´htcį ka´pac ti´lakaha = she has no chickens
ti´htcį ka´pac na´mu la´ka = she has lots of chickens
ka´pac ka´cku ti´laka = she's got some chickens
ka´pac ka´ckuto ti´laka = she's got several chickens
sa´k'uwan'a´ha = he doesn't want to eat
ti´htcį 'iyut'ä tila´k'aha = she has no hogs
sä´mą ma´sutak'ahtca = they going to turn it around
ta´yanici titci´r'ąta = they milk the cow
gave both forms, this time the one with the -t- first, at other times he gave the -k- form; he says the latter is better.)
mu´riku = something you fill up and pack it
mu´riwik'a´htca = he's going to fill it up (pack it full)
ti´cuhk'a´hkihtą ka´l'u´ra = he's standing back of the door
Deer and Turtle as Rivals (Terrapin Race)
becomes confused and says sometimes 'ɔsac n and sometimes 'ɔ´sak n
There used to be an Owl-mammoth.
Every night he (would) arrive and (when) he came into the village he (would) swallow two or three people [ every night ].
How They Used To Make Hats from Palmetto Leaves 31
How They Danced The " Coon " Dance 55 - 57
How and What They Used to Hunt 63 - 67
The Lord Tests Rabbit Three Times 101 - 137 (told a second time) (see II 87 - 115 for 1st telling)
The Origin of the Pole - Bean 143 - 155 (told a second time) (see II 147 - 157 for 1st telling)
Total number of pages in Vol.
